title: Uncoupled isotonic regression
speaker: Jonathan Niles-Weed
affiliation: New York University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: The classical regression problem seeks to estimate a function f on the basis of independent pairs $(x_i,y_i)$ where $\mathbb E[y_i]=f(x_i)$, $i=1,\dotsc,n$. In this talk, we consider statistical and computational aspects of the "uncoupled" version of this problem, where one observes only the unordered sets $\{x_1,…,x_n\}$ and $\{y_1,…,y_n\}$ and still hopes to recover information about $f$.

proof_stages.label: From paired regression to uncoupled samples
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from classical paired regression, motivates why paired labeled data may be unavailable, and formulates the uncoupled observation model in which only unordered collections of covariates and responses are observed, with monotonicity as the key identifiability assumption in one dimension.

proof_stages.label: Classical isotonic-regression benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the usual one-dimensional isotonic regression class of bounded nondecreasing functions with Gaussian noise and recalls that, with paired data, least-squares isotonic regression attains the minimax rate n^{-2/3} and is computable by the pool-adjacent-violators algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Main uncoupled minimax claim and assumptions
proof_stages.summary: The central problem is to determine rates and algorithms for uncoupled isotonic regression, and the announced result is that the minimax rate deteriorates to roughly log log n/(log n)^2 while remaining achievable by a simple projection-type estimator under known sub-exponential noise and bounded signal scale.

proof_stages.label: Wasserstein reformulation and projection estimator
proof_stages.summary: The empirical L2 distance between two increasing functions on the design points is identified with a W2 distance between their induced empirical measures, and the observed responses are recognized marginally as draws from the convolution of the true induced measure with the noise, leading to a minimum-Wasserstein-distance estimator.

proof_stages.label: Moment-based Wasserstein deconvolution upper bound
proof_stages.summary: The upper-bound analysis first obtains closeness after convolution by empirical Wasserstein concentration, then converts this to moment closeness, uses a theorem that moment gaps control Wp distance for sub-exponential measures, and finally deconvolves, with unavoidable exponential losses producing the logarithmic rate.

proof_stages.label: Two-point lower bound via blurred measures
proof_stages.summary: The minimax lower bound is obtained by constructing two measures that are well separated in Wasserstein distance but nearly indistinguishable in total variation after convolution with the noise, then turning them into random isotonic functions on arbitrary fixed design points.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-time computation and entropic-OT MLE
proof_stages.summary: Although the direct optimization over isotonic induced measures is nonconvex, relaxation to all probability measures, discretization, and rounding give a polynomial-time estimator, and an entropically regularized Wasserstein projection is identified with the maximum likelihood estimator.

proof_stages.label: Scope, extensions, and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion records that the analysis extends to sub-exponential noise and Wp/Lp losses and points to open problems involving mixtures of coupled and uncoupled data, multivariate identifiability, and adaptive sampling.
